Name: spectral_adaptive_layer_routing
Title: Spectral-Stable Online Layer Routing: PAC-Bayes-Bounded Inference with Uncertainty-Calibrated Jacobian Proxies
Short Hypothesis: We hypothesize that layer-skipping decisions at inference time can be grounded in PAC-Bayes stability bounds derived from per-layer Jacobian spectral norms, enabling training-free, latency-guaranteed routing with explicit uncertainty quantification. Unlike prior work that either treats layer importance as static (pruning) or uses learned routing modules (requiring retraining), we propose computing layer-wise routing decisions online via single-pass Jacobian norm estimation, bounded by spectral stability margins that formally guarantee bounded hidden-state perturbation under PAC-Bayes constraints. This directly addresses the gap: existing early-exit and layer-skipping methods lack rigorous perturbation bounds and incur variable latency; our approach couples mechanistic transparency (why layers are skipped) with strict, amortized wall-clock budgets.
Related Work: Early-exit networks (BranchyNet, BERT-Patient) and dynamic depth methods (Adaptive Computation Time) perform layer skipping but rely on learned confidence heads or heuristic thresholds without formal perturbation guarantees. Speculative decoding accelerates LLM inference via parallel draft models, but introduces memory overhead and is orthogonal to layer routing. Mixture-of-Experts routing (Switch Transformers, BASE) uses learned routers, requiring retraining or fine-tuning. Pruning-based methods (magnitude, SNIP, movement pruning) identify static layer importance offline but cannot adapt to per-instance latency budgets. PAC-Bayes theory has been applied to generalization bounds but rarely to inference-time routing with explicit latency constraints. Our work uniquely combines: (1) training-free, online Jacobian spectral estimation, (2) PAC-Bayes-derived stability margins linking perturbation bounds to layer-skip decisions, (3) amortized FLOPs accounting that maps spectral computation overhead to wall-clock budgets, (4) single-pass sequential routing (no parallel sampling), and (5) uncertainty-calibrated proxies for online threshold adaptation without full covariance estimation.
Abstract: Inference-time layer skipping offers a natural mechanism for latency-adaptive neural network execution, yet current methods either lack formal perturbation guarantees or incur unpredictable computational overhead. We propose Spectral-Stable Online Layer Routing (SOLAR), a training-free inference framework that grounds layer-skip decisions in PAC-Bayes stability bounds derived from per-layer Jacobian spectral norms. SOLAR operates via three stages: (1) lightweight, single-pass Jacobian norm estimation per layer using input-output covariance proxies, (2) online threshold adaptation via uncertainty-calibrated margin computation that enforces PAC-Bayes perturbation bounds, and (3) sequential routing decisions that respect strict wall-clock latency budgets by amortizing spectral computation into a bounded-overhead protocol. Unlike early-exit methods that use learned confidence heads, SOLAR requires no retraining; unlike static pruning, it adapts per-instance. We derive explicit spectral stability radii linking layer Frobenius norms to hidden-state perturbation under the assumption that layer skipping induces additive noise; this radius directly determines skip thresholds. Crucially, we rigorously account for spectral estimation overhead (FLOPs and memory bandwidth) as a fraction of total inference latency, replacing zero-overhead claims with quantified bounds. Experiments on Vision Transformers and BERT-scale models demonstrate that SOLAR achieves Pareto-optimal throughput-accuracy trade-offs comparable to learned routing, while providing formal perturbation guarantees and enabling per-instance latency budgets. We validate mechanistic transparency via ablations isolating Jacobian estimation, margin calibration, and threshold adaptation, and provide exact hardware telemetry across CPU and GPU inference.

Experiments.name: Experiment 1: Spectral Norm Estimation Accuracy and Overhead
Experiments.description: Validate the core proxy: per-layer Jacobian spectral norm estimation via input-output covariance. For a held-out test set (ImageNet-1K validation, 1000 samples for ViT-Base; MNLI dev set, 500 samples for BERT-base), compute: (a) true spectral norms via power iteration (5 iterations, amortized into offline ablation), (b) proxy spectral norms via single-pass activation covariance rank-1 approximation (Hutchinson trace estimator with 1 sample), (c) wall-clock latency of proxy computation per layer (ms on single GPU/CPU). Metric: Pearson correlation ρ between true and proxy norms; FLOPs and memory bandwidth of proxy per layer as percentage of forward pass. Target: ρ > 0.85, overhead < 5% wall-clock time. Report per-layer variance across samples to quantify proxy uncertainty.
Experiments.algorithmic_detail: For layer ℓ with input x_ℓ ∈ R^{d_in} and output y_ℓ = f_ℓ(x_ℓ) ∈ R^{d_out}, compute Jacobian norm proxy as: σ̂_ℓ = √(trace(Cov(y_ℓ)) / trace(Cov(x_ℓ))), where covariance is estimated over a batch or single sample with exponential moving average. Compare to true σ_ℓ = max_i σ_i(∂f_ℓ/∂x_ℓ) via power iteration.

Experiments.name: Experiment 2: PAC-Bayes Perturbation Bound Calibration
Experiments.description: Derive and empirically validate PAC-Bayes stability margins. For each layer ℓ, compute PAC-Bayes bound on perturbation radius: given spectral norm σ̂_ℓ and skip probability p_ℓ, the expected hidden-state perturbation is bounded by Δ_ℓ(p_ℓ, σ̂_ℓ) = p_ℓ · σ̂_ℓ · ||x_ℓ||_2 (under Gaussian noise assumption). Set skip threshold τ_ℓ such that cumulative perturbation ∑_ℓ Δ_ℓ ≤ ε (user-specified, e.g., ε = 0.1). Measure: (a) empirical hidden-state perturbation when skipping layer ℓ (L2 distance between full forward and skip-forward hidden states), (b) whether empirical perturbation stays within PAC-Bayes bound, (c) correlation between bound tightness and actual accuracy drop. Across ViT-Base and BERT-base, vary ε ∈ {0.05, 0.1, 0.2, 0.5} and report accuracy retention and skip rates.
Experiments.algorithmic_detail: Skip layer ℓ if σ̂_ℓ < τ_ℓ(ε), where τ_ℓ is computed as: τ_ℓ = ε / (p_skip · ||x_ℓ||_2), and p_skip is the empirical frequency of skips in a calibration set. Compute cumulative perturbation bound as product: ∏_ℓ (1 + Δ_ℓ) ≈ 1 + ∑_ℓ Δ_ℓ under small-perturbation regime.

Experiments.name: Experiment 3: Online Threshold Adaptation and Latency Budget Enforcement
Experiments.description: Test adaptive routing under strict wall-clock latency budgets. Implement sequential routing: for each test sample, compute spectral proxies layer-by-layer; at each layer, decide skip/execute based on current cumulative latency vs. remaining budget. Latency budget B (ms) is set to fractions of baseline full inference: B ∈ {50%, 60%, 70%, 80%, 90%} of full latency. Metric: (a) percentage of samples meeting latency budget (hard constraint), (b) accuracy achieved, (c) actual wall-clock time distribution (mean, p95, p99), (d) variance in latency across samples (coefficient of variation). Compare against: (i) uniform layer skipping (skip every k-th layer), (ii) learned early-exit baseline (if available, e.g., BERT-Patient), (iii) static magnitude pruning (skip layers with smallest average activation norm). Report on ImageNet-1K (ViT-Base, 5K test samples) and MNLI (BERT-base, 1K test samples).
Experiments.algorithmic_detail: At layer ℓ, current cumulative time is t_curr; remaining budget is B - t_curr. Compute time to execute layer ℓ as t_ℓ (measured offline). Skip if σ̂_ℓ < τ_ℓ AND t_curr + t_ℓ > B - ε_slack (where ε_slack = 5ms buffer). Update τ_ℓ adaptively: τ_ℓ(t) = τ_ℓ(t-1) · (1 + η · (t_curr - B)_+), where η is a small learning rate (e.g., 0.01) and (·)_+ is positive part.

Experiments.name: Experiment 4: Mechanistic Transparency via Ablation and Gradient Conflict Probing
Experiments.description: Isolate contributions of (a) Jacobian proxy quality, (b) PAC-Bayes margin calibration, (c) uncertainty-calibrated threshold adaptation. Ablations: (i) replace spectral proxy with random skip decisions (baseline), (ii) use true spectral norms (oracle), (iii) use fixed thresholds (no adaptation), (iv) use learned routing head (retraining required, for comparison only). For each ablation, report accuracy, latency, and skip rate on ViT-Base ImageNet validation (1K samples). Additionally, use deterministic projection-based probes: for layers that are frequently skipped, compute gradient variance w.r.t. skip decision; for layers with high gradient variance, verify that PAC-Bayes bounds are tight (i.e., actual perturbation ≈ bound). Identify layers where gradient conflicts (opposing skip/execute signals) occur and document via scatter plot of (σ̂_ℓ, accuracy_impact_ℓ).
Experiments.algorithmic_detail: Gradient conflict probe: for layer ℓ, compute ∂L/∂(skip_ℓ) via finite differences (perturb skip probability by ±ε), and check if sign is consistent across samples. High variance in sign indicates conflict. Plot (σ̂_ℓ, var(∂L/∂skip_ℓ)) to identify problematic layers.

Experiments.name: Experiment 5: Throughput-Accuracy Pareto Frontier and Cross-Hardware Validation
Experiments.description: Construct Pareto frontiers of (latency, accuracy) by sweeping latency budgets B. Run SOLAR on three hardware targets: (i) single V100 GPU (inference mode, batch size 1), (ii) single A100 GPU (batch size 1 and 8), (iii) CPU (Intel Xeon, single-threaded). For each hardware-batch combination, measure actual wall-clock time (5 runs per sample, report mean ± std). Plot accuracy vs. latency (ms) for SOLAR, uniform layer skipping, and learned baselines. Compute Pareto efficiency: percentage of SOLAR points on Pareto frontier. Validate that amortized spectral overhead stays < 5% on all hardware targets.
Experiments.algorithmic_detail: Latency budget sweep: B ∈ {50%, 55%, 60%, ..., 95%} of full inference latency. For each B, run SOLAR on 1000 samples, measure accuracy and wall-clock latency (using time.perf_counter() on CPU, torch.cuda.synchronize() + timing on GPU). Report Pareto frontier as convex hull of (latency, accuracy) points.

Experiments.name: Experiment 6: Robustness to Distribution Shift and Structured Perturbations
Experiments.description: Test stability under domain shift and adversarial perturbations. Evaluate SOLAR on: (a) ImageNet-C (corruption types: brightness, contrast, noise, blur; 5 severity levels), (b) BERT on out-of-distribution text (e.g., Reddit comments vs. Wikipedia), (c) adversarial perturbations (FGSM, ε ∈ {4/255, 8/255, 16/255}). For each perturbation type, measure: (i) change in skip decisions (% of layers with different skip status), (ii) accuracy drop, (iii) latency variance. Compute stability radius: maximum ε such that skip decisions remain stable. Report stability radius for each layer and overall model.
Experiments.algorithmic_detail: For adversarial perturbations, compute FGSM: x_adv = x + ε · sign(∇_x L(f(x), y)). For each perturbed sample, run SOLAR and compare skip decisions to clean sample. Stability radius is defined as max{ε : Pr[skip_ℓ(x_adv) = skip_ℓ(x)] > 0.9 for all ℓ}.

Experiments.name: Experiment 7: Multi-Seed Variance Quantification and Reproducibility
Experiments.description: Ensure reproducibility and quantify stochasticity. Run all experiments with 5 random seeds (different data orderings, initialization of moving averages). Report mean ± std (95% CI) for all metrics: accuracy, latency, skip rate, correlation ρ. Provide exact hardware specifications (GPU model, driver version, CUDA version, CPU model, clock speed) and full hyperparameter configurations (learning rates, batch sizes, number of power iterations, etc.). Release code and raw telemetry (per-sample latency, skip decisions, spectral norms) as supplementary material.
Experiments.algorithmic_detail: For each seed s ∈ {1,2,3,4,5}, shuffle test set, reinitialize exponential moving average, and re-run Experiment 3 and 5. Compute mean accuracy and latency across seeds; report 95% confidence intervals.

Risk Factors and Limitations.risk: Jacobian norm estimation via covariance proxy may be inaccurate for layers with low-rank activations or highly non-linear behavior. Rank-1 approximation (Hutchinson trace estimator with 1 sample) could underestimate true spectral norms.
Risk Factors and Limitations.mitigation: Experiment 1 directly validates proxy accuracy (target ρ > 0.85). If ρ < 0.85, increase sample budget for covariance estimation or use higher-rank approximations (SVD with k=2 or 3), accepting higher FLOPs overhead.

Risk Factors and Limitations.risk: PAC-Bayes bounds assume Gaussian noise model for layer skipping (additive perturbation), which may not hold if skip induces multiplicative or structural changes to hidden states.
Risk Factors and Limitations.mitigation: Experiment 2 empirically validates bound tightness by measuring actual hidden-state perturbation. If bounds are loose (actual perturbation << bound), relax ε to improve skip rates. If bounds are violated, switch to empirical perturbation estimates instead of theory-derived bounds.

Risk Factors and Limitations.risk: Online threshold adaptation (Experiment 3) may be unstable if latency budget is very tight (B << full latency), leading to excessive layer skipping and accuracy collapse.
Risk Factors and Limitations.mitigation: Enforce minimum skip rate or maximum cumulative perturbation to prevent collapse. Set ε_slack buffer to prevent last-minute budget overruns. If instability persists, fall back to fixed thresholds calibrated offline.

Risk Factors and Limitations.risk: Spectral estimation overhead (FLOPs, memory bandwidth) may exceed 5% budget on CPU or memory-constrained devices, violating latency guarantees.
Risk Factors and Limitations.mitigation: Experiment 1 and 5 measure overhead on all hardware targets. If overhead exceeds 5%, amortize computation across multiple samples (batch processing) or cache spectral norms (if input distribution is stable).

Risk Factors and Limitations.risk: Single-pass Jacobian estimation may miss layer-to-layer interactions; skip decisions based on local spectral norms may not account for global model behavior.
Risk Factors and Limitations.mitigation: Experiment 4 (ablation) compares against oracle (true spectral norms) to quantify loss from local approximation. If gap is large, incorporate pairwise layer correlations (requires offline covariance estimation, reserved for ablation).

Risk Factors and Limitations.risk: Generalization to very deep models (e.g., ResNet-152, GPT-3 scale) is unclear; spectral norms may become uninformative in extremely deep networks.
Risk Factors and Limitations.mitigation: Scope experiments to ViT-Base (12 layers) and BERT-base (12 layers) initially. Future work can extend to deeper models with hierarchical spectral estimation (e.g., per-block aggregation).

Risk Factors and Limitations.risk: Learned early-exit baselines (e.g., BERT-Patient) may already be well-optimized, making it difficult to show clear advantages for SOLAR.
Risk Factors and Limitations.mitigation: Emphasize advantages of SOLAR: training-free (no retraining), formal perturbation bounds, per-instance latency budgets. If SOLAR is not clearly better in accuracy, focus on latency predictability and mechanistic transparency as differentiators.

Risk Factors and Limitations.risk: Reproducibility may be hindered by hardware variability (GPU thermal throttling, OS scheduling) affecting wall-clock time measurements.
Risk Factors and Limitations.mitigation: Run multiple seeds (Experiment 7), report variance in latency, and use high-precision timing (torch.cuda.synchronize() on GPU, perf counters on CPU). Disable dynamic frequency scaling if possible. Report exact hardware configuration and environmental conditions.
